items.q: According to the inverted-U hypothesis, when is performance best?
items.correct: At an optimal moderate level of arousal.
items.why: Students assume 'more psyched up = better'. The inverted-U shows performance rises with arousal up to an optimum, then DECLINES with excess arousal (the athlete chokes). The optimum varies by skill.

items.q: What is the difference between aerobic and muscular endurance?
items.correct: Aerobic endurance is the cardiovascular system's ability to supply oxygen; muscular endurance is a muscle's ability to contract repeatedly without fatigue.
items.misconception: They are different names for the same component.
items.random_wrong: Aerobic is for legs, muscular for arms.
items.why: Students treat both as 'being able to keep going'. Aerobic endurance is WHOLE-BODY cardio capacity; muscular endurance is LOCALISED to specific muscles (e.g., sit-up reps).
Students confuse storage with circulation. Blood carries glucose; muscles and liver store glycogen (the storage form). Liver glycogen regulates blood sugar; muscle glycogen fuels local contraction.

items.q: How many cervical vertebrae are there?
items.why: It's 7. “12” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.
It's Bone adaptation to load. “Lung volumes” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Total vertebrae in the human spine?
items.why: The real figure is 33 vertebrae. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
